chunk_summary: This section outlines the minimum data portability requirements for tele-nutrition in India, focusing on standardized data export for longitudinal growth and nutrition care to ensure clinical safety and interoperability.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of data portability in longitudinal pediatric nutrition care.
Identify the three key bundles required for standardized tele-nutrition data export.
Learn how to implement de-duplication rules to minimize identity errors in shared-phone households.
Explore the role of FHIR standards in tele-nutrition data interoperability.
key_facts: Data portability prevents rework, misclassification, and ensures medico-legal defensibility in pediatric care.
The minimum data packet includes three bundles: growth and measurement, clinical decisions, and plan/versioning.
Growth data must include anthropometry with units, methods, confidence grades, and locked growth references to prevent replotting errors.
De-duplication rules should match by ABHA or child name + DOB + caregiver phone, flagging near-matches for manual review.
Tele-nutrition tools must support one-click export in structured formats like FHIR bundles.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Electronic Health Record Standards for India, 2016: 'Data portability is essential for ensuring continuity of care and interoperability across health systems.'
document_key_topics: Standardized export for longitudinal growth care
De-duplication and identity matching in shared-phone households
